A useful summary, but I didn’t see anything new there.

Also, he didn’t elaborate on his reference to “some unfortunate decisions about the quality of corks.”

I haven’t followed individual producers’ track records, so I’m curious: Is there anyone with bad premox problems who didn’t use some combination of gentler pressing, lees stirring and reduced sulfur?

In other words, is it really clear now that it’s some combination of those things that’s at the root of the problem? Or are they still experimenting with their fingers crossed?

Herwig Janssen:

Very interesting Ryan. Oxidizing the must is something JF Coche has always done … Maybe that’s one of the reasons his wines very rarely premox .

I remember seeing oxidized chardonnay must at Matanzas Creek in the early 90s. They swore by it!

posts.name: larry schaffer
posts.cooked:
I think one of the things that has been discussed with corks is the past was the use of heavily ‘bleached’ corks around this time - and the use of hydrogen peroxide to do so. The corks were made to look a lot ‘cleaner’, but hydrogen peroxide is an oxidizer . . . and therefore the thought is that if these were used, there was potential for this peroxide to get into the wine, eating up free SO2 much faster and therefore leading to oxidation issues faster.

Cheers.

posts.name: Eric Lundblad
posts.cooked:
In addition to the prior sins list of ‘gentler pressing, lees stirring, reduced sulfur, not preoxidizing the must and high/variable oxtrans of corks’, the article proposes longer times in barrel (being a problem).

Also, in Remington Norman’s Great Domaines of Burgundy (Second Edition, printed 1996), Remington said JF Coche believes in adding sulfur to the grapes as soon as they are picked to avoid any possibility of oxidation. I assume from this that, at that time at least, he didn’t believe in preoxidizing the must. It also says he stirred the lees.

I remember seeing oxidized chardonnay must at Matanzas Creek in the early 90s. They swore by it!

I’m seeing more and more new world producers experimenting with the must browning technique. When I visited Cooper-Garrod in March, the winemaker had me blind taste two Chardonnay barrel samples. Both were quite similar, but the 2nd sample seemed more complex and better integrated. He told me that for the 2016 vintage, they browned half of their Chardonnay and I had preferred the sample which had undergone the technique. An eye-opening experience that made me a believer.

posts.name: Mel Knox
posts.cooked:
Somebody posted an article by Denis Dubordieu and Avril Lavigne on this subject. They suggested that the culprit is the lack of glutathione in the wine. Glutathione is a powerful anti oxidant found in lees. The new presses eliminate a lot of lees.

Chardonnay was made w/o must browning here in the '70s and some wines aged well…lots of SO2 was involved. Most Burgundians understand this issue. Indeed I once took a vigneron from Meursault to visit somebody around 1984. The Californian explained why now he was letting the juice brown and explained the science behind it. The Burgundian was quite interested, said he had always wondered why his juice browned and then cleared up.

Speaking of corks, I noted that my last shipment of Hospices de Beaune wine was bottled with Diam corks, but I am not sure what the reasoning was…cork taint?? premox??

If longer time in barrel is an issue, then why do Chardonnays made in the '80s and '90s at wineries like Chalone and Au Bon Climat age so well?? They were usually bottled after 18 months in barrel.

posts.name: Stuart BeauneHead Niemtzow
posts.cooked:
And, as always, take a look at the corks on premoxed bottles. They are usually hard as rocks; coated often with silicone or some other lubricant. Hard to imagine they make a consistently good seal from bottle to bottle.

Then, look at good bottles, particularly older ones…where the corks are soft and spongy. Good for making good seals.

The wines are more vulnerable…probably. But, many bottles are, nevertheless, fine. The only variable is the seal.

Just read the article: nothing new. This problem won’t be solved by artisanal winemakers chewing the fat together. That’s been tried for the last 20 years now. And, these guys don’t even mention corks. The bad bottles are the ones to focus on. Why change the wine…if most bottles are fine at 20 years…IF they are? Figure out why the bad ones are bad, ie, the variables. It might not be the wines at all.

I have always wondered if the high scores Parker gave to Verget’s 1992 wines had anything to do with this. The article makes it sound like it:

“Many gravitated to the pneumatic presses coming into fashion at the time, which promised to treat grapes more gently, and sought to minimize the contact between grape skins and juice. Some dramatically reduced the amount of the preservative sulfur dioxide in the wines, hoping to make them more approachable. And they often aggressively stirred the lees in barrels, a technique that could bolster the texture of what might otherwise have been anemic, acidic wines. The technique defined the buxom California style popular at the time, and it continued to be used in Burgundy even as more careful farming delivered riper, healthier, more concentrated fruit that did not necessarily need as much lees-inflected enrichment.”

Wasn’t Guffens a pioneer in using some of these techniques in the Cotes du Beaune.

posts.name: Ed Kurtzman
posts.cooked: I was glad to see Jon mention that Burgundians are starting to crush the Chardonnay pre-pressing again to get more skin contact. I’ve been advocating this for years since I believe it was the single biggest change in the early '90s, that whole cluster pressing (minimal skin contact) led to premox more than any other factor. I tried researching the history of whole cluster pressing, but didn’t get very far. The Burgundians I asked didn’t really want to talk about it. I did get some good answers from Californian winemakers who were making Chard from before whole cluster pressing through today. They say it started in the late '80s and early '90s, and by 1994-1995, everyone was doing it. I was trying to find out when Burgundians made the switch, and I can only guess it was around the same time.

posts.name: Mel Knox
posts.cooked:
I noticed three changes in the mid to late 90s in the making of white Burgundy:
1/less SO2
2/a quick jump from old fashioned plate presses to modern tank presses
3/warmer harvests…a trend that continues unabated.

If you mix less SO2 with riper musts, then right away you are asking for trouble.
How the new presses fit in…this confused me…what happened here?
I kept thinking about the must browning issue, but I overlooked the key point of the new presses: less solids, less lees, less glutathione.

Perhaps the issues that Ed K brings up also apply. I don’t know.

Jon Bonne’s point that you cannot have early drinking white Burgundies that age well is a good one.

I read past the barrel/tank aging thing too quickly. I’ve read similar comments from a couple of burgundians. Both had lengthened their elevage, by adding time in tank, but the time in barrels had been shortened…so I took Jon’s comments in this spirit.

If you increase the structure of a white, then more time in barrel would be equivalent to a less structured wine spending less time in barrel. So the comments Jon made about the wine spending more time in barrel makes sense because of the extra structure that are discussed (foot stomping, harder press, picking for acid, reduced yields, etc). However, more time in barrel, all other things being equal, as an anti-premox measure isn’t plausible. Time in tank is an interesting/worthwhile idea, but is more akin to time in bottle than time in barrel (unless micro ox is used…ridiculous in this case).

Thanks for pointing that out…did I miss anything else?


Age worthy CA Chards: I agree with Mt Eden, ABC, Stony Hill & Kalin. I’ve had 15-20 yo Littorai, Calera and Marcassin Chards that were youthful + appropriate/interesting aged character. About 10 years ago, I had a 79 Kistler chard…I didn’t know they were making wine back then, their first year apparently. The wine appeared completely dead when opened. Promptly ignored, I tried it an hour later and it completely transformed inself into a correct/interesting/compelling wine. Hard to know what to make of that, other than don’t count a bottle of wine out too quickly. The top of these, for me, is Mt Eden…a 90 Mt Eden chard a while back was breathtaking and world class. There are other 15+ yo CA chards I’ve had/liked, but don’t recall them atm.

2/people have been blaming corks for premox since around 2005…you would think things would have shifted a bit since then

Also, Dr Lavigne’s first name is Valerie, not Avril…

I agree. To blame the corks was an easy way to shift the blame. If corks were the issue, premox would be an issue in wines from all over the world, not just one region. Doesn’t take a rocket scientist to figure that one out. I’ve been saying that for years. This quote in the article, IMO, sums up where the issue came from. I am surprised someone actually admitted it, even though Moreau did so without actually saying they screwed up…

Corks are the only explanation for good ones and bad ones being in the same case of wine. There is no other variable.

The “cork” issue is not what they’re treated with in the sense of a chemical reaction, but what they’re treated with to make them easier to pull out…rendering some of them rigid and unable to make a good seal with the bottle neck.

I’ve recently started wondering…maybe the extent of the problem is the bad bottles…and the rest have been fine. We’re focusing on the wine’s composition rather than the difference in good and bad bottles and assuming a universal problem with the wine…when it could be as simple as bad seals let out the protective SO2 in some bottles…and they premox. Nothing wrong with the wine, per se.

So, rejecting corks as a potential culprit, when they are the only variable, seems close minded…and maybe why “they” haven’t solved the problem in the 20 years since it started…“they” assume all the wines are bad and only some get prem-oxed. Maybe all the wines are fine and the bad ones, with bad sealing corks, are the extent of the problem.

I can’t argue for it…but I sure can argue why the corks cannot be dismissed as part…or all…of the cause of the problem.

Stuart BeauneHead Niemtzow:

Corks are the only explanation for good ones and bad ones being in the same case of wine. There is no other variable.

I suspect this is true, though it would be sensible for them to eliminate other possibilities of variation (between bottles from the same barrel) e.g. imprecision in SO2 additions / headspace etc.

However it then comes down to what makes a premox causing cork? Is it greater permeability, more residue from the bleaching process or other treatment, differing quality of ‘seal’ when in contact with the neck, etc.

It is also feasible that winemaking decisions make the effect even more marked, so rather than shaving a few years off when the cork wasn’t ideal, it’s now resulting in the catastrophic failure of affected wines. Reduced SO2 additions would be one such decision.

It is very difficult though to see the variability explained by anything but the cork, and working backwards from there seems confusingly obvious.

posts.name: John Morris
posts.cooked:
Larry Link:

If corks are the issue then why don’t we see the same premox rates in red wines? They use the same corks after all.

For starters, with reds, the juice stays in contact with the skins for extended periods of time, so it picks up all sorts of things, including tannins, that act as preservatives. That’s why, in general, reds age longer than whites (old white Burgundy and sweet wines being exceptions to that general rule).

Most red wines are less vulnerable to oxygen exposure and oxidation than most white wines, which means that it will take longer for adverse effects to be noticeable in red wines, but it is IMO not without reason that older red wines are not safe bets. ‘There are no good old wines just good old individual bottles’ or similar statements are well known.

I’ve encountered many premoxed red wines. Some red wines are IMO more vulnerable than others. Some of the red 2003 wines from Chateauneuf du Pape (very ripe and some wines with low SO2) were for example hit by an above average premox rate.

The cork is still IMO the only plausible explanation for random premox. Many other effects (such as global warming, other presses, less SO2 etc) could cause wines to be more vulnerable to oxidation in general, but the huge variation in air permeability between corks is still the only explanation that can lead one bottle in a case of wine to age much quicker than another bottle in the same case. Please note that there are several studies that have shown that the difference in air permeability between corks in the same batch of corks could range from 10x to over 1000x.

That’s not true. There can be a huge variance in the amount of oxygen in the headspace coming off the bottling line. Jamie Goode mentioned this in an article: https://www.winesandvines.com/template.cfm?section=features&content=66003&ftitle=OXYGEN%20%20%26%20WINE

posts.name: Don Cornwell
posts.cooked:
I think it is naive to declare, as the article does, that this is the “post-premox” era. In my view, that’s plainly false. Premox on the better wines generally doesn’t start showing up until approximately the seventh year after the vintage. (Though yes, for some producers who have consistent oxidation problems, such as Fontaine-Gagnard, Blain-Gagnard, Matrot, Mikulski and some others it frequently occurs sooner). A group of us have been annually tasting most of the top wines from each decent vintage when they are 7.5 years old. The percentage of wines which exhibit either clearly advanced or outright oxidized characteristics at 7.5 years of age is still unacceptably high. While the 2002 to 2009 vintages have had a slightly lower average percentage of affected bottles than the 1996 to 2001 vintages did, the three best vintages we’ve ever experienced still had 13% of the wines judged either as advanced or outright oxidized. 2005 was the worst vintage ever with nearly 31% of the wines being advanced or oxidized.

Overall, the most recent vintage results appear to be slightly better, but the results among the producers are clearly mixed.

There are certainly producers who were formerly poster children for premox that have dramatically improved their games since the mid-1990s, including Montille, Sauzet, Jean-Marc Pillot and Jacques Carillon. But there are other producers who initially had no serious oxidation issues in the 1990s that subsequently completely fell apart and became some of the worst premox offenders (e.g. Jadot starting with 2000, and Leflaive starting with either 2002 or 2005 depending on your viewpoint.)

Solving the problem has to start with having a well-controlled seal on the bottle with a consistent oxygen transmission rate (otherwise you have widely varying performance within cases of wine as we’ve all experienced.) A lot of producers have spent time and money pursuing better quality corks, and in some cases slightly larger diameter corks, in the hope of solving this problem. One of the things that I thought was a simple but brilliant solution by Philippe Prost at Bouchard was to weigh the corks of his grand crus (as proxy measure of cork density) and to reject those that were too light. However a number of producers, including some of the worst premox offenders, have elected to go with DIAM closures as a means of having very controlled and uniform oxygen transmission rates. 2009 was the first vintage where DIAM closures appeared on the best bottles and there are more producers making the conversion each vintage.

We tasted nine bottles of 2009 whites with DIAM closures and none of them exhibited any advanced or oxidized characteristics (and similarly no off or unexpected aromas or flavors either.) I think this is an encouraging sign, but the sample is still too small at this point to draw any definitive conclusions. Things should be interesting over the coming vintages as some of the worst premox offenders started bottling all of their wines under DIAM – Fevre’s grand crus were bottled under DIAM starting with 2010. Jadot’s whites were bottled with DIAM starting in 2011. Lafon converted to DIAM with the 2013 vintage and Domaine Leflaive converted to DIAM starting with the 2014 vintage. If as we start tasting those producers wines under DIAM and premox disappears, then I think we’ll be a lot closer to declaring victory.

Many producers have restored their SO2 usage to former levels or increased it to relatively high levels as a means of providing longer life for their wines. Based on the experience of Carillon and Sauzet, this definitely seems to work well.

A number of producers have also taken steps to reduce dissolved oxygen that had been injected into the bottles by their automated bottling lines. I’m still agnostic about this change.

Doug Schulman:
Stuart BeauneHead Niemtzow:

Corks are the only explanation for good ones and bad ones being in the same case of wine. There is no other variable.

That’s not true. There can be a huge variance in the amount of oxygen in the headspace coming off the bottling line. Jamie Goode mentioned this in an article: [url][Wine Business Analytics]

I think it is the only plausible explanation for variability.

There has been way more random ullage with WB in past years and there was no such problem, random or otherwise. (Some WB from the '70s and early '80s were also all over the place re: ullage. In fact, some of the best bottles I’ve had of older WB have had large ullage (neck space between the wine and the bottom of the cork.)

And, I’m starting to believe that corks might be the only explanation. Period. How else do you explain a perfectly excellent 1999 Niellon 1er cru Chassagne that I opened this morning for dinner tonight…and tasted. Different kinds of wines in different bottles? Isn’t it possible that the wines are fine…and the only premox is from the bottles we find oxidized…ie, the “random” bottles. In other words, isn’t it possible that the “random” bottles within any given case…batch…winery…are the full extent of the “premox” problem?

Why not?

I’m asking this rhetorical questions because, in the 20+ years this problem has been rampant on the Cote de Beaune…NO ONE has ever come up with a solution that involved the wine, itself. In fact, the most plausible solution is figuring out how to keep the protective SO2 inside the bottle, IMO. That people have been ascribing the problem to the wine, rather than the cork closure (not the chemicals on the cork) , to me, might just be the reason no more plausible reason has ever been proven and no changes have solved the problem. Maybe “they” are just unwilling to believe that it is a simple issue: inept closures allowing protective SO2 to leave the wine. If we all agree that the integrity of the closure is the only (or most likely) explanation for the randomness of the premox, why can’t it also be the entire explanation for the “premox”…and those bottles the entire roster of wines so affected? (Which means the cause of the randomness is the cause of the problem.) This is basically a rhetorical question, but…I’d love to hear some answers to it.

p.s. FWIW, the cork I pulled was clearly a silicone-coated one. The end at the top of the bottle was dry and rigid. The end at the bottom of the cork was sponge-like and wet, just as any cork should be.

And, yes I acknowledge that modern “improvements” have made the wines more vulnerable by taking some of the protective chemicals out of the mix. But, that doesn’t mean that the wines are so vulnerable that they can’t last 20 years or more. The one I opened is 17+ years…and extremely vibrant.

To be clear, I’m not arguing for the corks as the only explanation. But, I’m asking “why” it can’t be the only explanation?

posts.cooked:
It’s clearly not JUST the corks that create the problem I can demonstrate that for you with a couple of examples.

First, let’s take Henri Boillot as an example. Boillot, who I suspect varies his level of SO2 used based upon his particular “feelings” about the wine (rather than type of scientific or laboratory measurement) has horrific variation in oxidation rates on his wines despite using the same corks on all of his top bottlings. For example, I’ve so far opened 10 bottles of 2007 Henri Boillot Corton Charlemagne from three different six packs between January of 2015 and the present. Nine of them have been fully oxidized and one opened about four months ago was fully mature and not long for this world. On the other other hand, the other 2007 Boillot wines I’ve opened to date (Batard, Chevalier, Meursault Perrieres and Puligny Clos de Moucheres) have all been okay – though a Chevalier opened last week was past its prime and about to go. And it’s not Boillot’s Corton vineyard that’s at fault either. On the 2008 Corton Charley I’ve now consumed 9 of the 12 bottles I bought and those are all in superb shape and very fresh and bright today. Sadly, I’ve experienced this frequently with Boillot over the years – when he has a premox problem it’s generally not random bottle variation, instead when you experience a premox problem with one of his bottlings it is likely to affect all or nearly all of the bottles of that particular bottling.

Another example that proves the point is Fontaine-Gagnard. Open any bottle you want at seven or more years and you’ll have about an 90% chance that the wine will be premoxed. Change the vintage or the bottling and it won’t make any difference. I could say the same for a number of other producers including Blain-Gagnard, Matrot and Mikulski.

There are also producers who for over a decade have had virtually no premox problems, e.g. Raveneau, Leroy, Coche-Dury and (since 1996) Roulot. Does anyone really believe that each of these producers has some secret source/supply of corks unique to them that explains why they don’t have virtually any premox problems?

Or how about the producers whose wines have exhibited night and day differences in premox performance over the years Here I have Jadot and Domaine Leflaive particularly in mind. In 1995, 1996 and 1999 Jadot didn’t have ANY premox problems, but in 2000 they turned on like a faucet and it’s been a flood ever since. Jadot is one of those producers who admitted deliberately changing their style to try to make wines that were much more ready to drink on release. For another example of this, how about Domaine Leflaive? No premox problems at all from 1995 through 2001, then starting in 2002 there was notable percentage of bottles that turned out to be premoxed, and then in 2006, 2007, 2008 and 2009 the roof fell in and Leflaive was suddenly one of the worst performers among all producers.

posts.cooked:
Don Cornwell:

It’s clearly not JUST the corks that create the problem I can demonstrate that for you with a couple of examples.

First, let’s take Henri Boillot as an example. Boillot, who I suspect varies his level of SO2 used based upon his particular “feelings” about the wine (rather than type of scientific or laboratory measurement) has horrific variation in oxidation rates on his wines despite using the same corks on all of his top bottlings. For example, I’ve so far opened 10 bottles of 2007 Henri Boillot Corton Charlemagne from three different six packs between January of 2015 and the present. Nine of them have been fully oxidized and one opened about four months ago was fully mature and not long for this world. On the other other hand, the other 2007 Boillot wines I’ve opened to date (Batard, Chevalier, Meursault Perrieres and Puligny Clos de Moucheres) have all been okay – though a Chevalier opened last week was past its prime and about to go. And it’s not Boillot’s Corton vineyard that’s at fault either. On the 2008 Corton Charley I’ve now consumed 9 of the 12 bottles I bought and those are all in superb shape and very fresh and bright today. Sadly, I’ve experienced this frequently with Boillot over the years – when he has a premox problem it’s generally not random bottle variation, instead when you experience a premox problem with one of his bottlings it is likely to affect all or nearly all of the bottles of that particular bottling.

Don, with due respect…I don’t think you’ve demonstrated that. I’m not arguing for the supposition that the corks are the ONLY issue/variable, but with your contention that you’ve just proved it wrong.

When I say “corks” I really mean “closures” because to make a closure…and keep in the protective SO2…the cork works in conjunction with the bottle neck, as Mel pointed out. My thought is that poor seals let out the SO2 in affected bottles…and the rest of the bottles are fine.
Your assumption is that all corks within a batch/load, whatever are the same. I think that each individual cork, with both silicone and parrafin on them are like fingerprints…no two are identical, because of the application method combined with using a natural, irregular thing like the bark of a cork tree to plug the neck. That’s why I think it’s so random…even if, as you posit Boillot “used the same corks on all of his top bottlings.”. Same doesn’t mean identical…it just means that they were sold in the same batch or sale. So, that origin…or same “model number” doesn’t mean that they are all equally capable of a good seal.

I saw that with the wonderful bottle of Niellon 1er cru 1999 I had last night. One end of the cork was supremly rigid. The bottom end was very malleable and wet. The wine blossomed from last night into a honeyed, minerally great example of a “mature” (at 17 years) WB. Even better today…just like most Dauvissat Chablis are. Others I’ve had from this cru and year…were not good, my notes say. One was “strange” ; another oxidized.

This thread is a really good one. When I have some time…I’m going to look into the method silicone is applied (I assume in most cases also using some parrafin.) That could cause supreme variablity within any batch of corks; the cork bark itself has to react in unique ways…like fingerprints…to the process. A rigid silicone will obviously make a better seal with a more rigid cork…and less good with an irregular cork.

Finally…and I’m in the middle of preparing for an appellate court argument next week…so…I did see, I think on your Wiki site…but via a google search yesterday…the permeability of silicone is great; parrafin among the best impervious coatings. So…it is plausibe that the change from solely parrafin to either soley silicone or a mixture…which your site, I think said happened in 1995…changed the ability of SO2 to escape at the time of the change. So, it’s a double issue: permeability and malleability, both of which affect the the corks and , therefore, the seals.

Something I do remember from a visit in 2004? 2007? is that most winemakers I asked had no idea the coating was even changed from all parrafin, though they wanted to avoid “silicone” by then. I don’t think anyone before that focused on the corks issues…other than for TCA issues.

So…I have some research to do…on the permeability issue…and on the method the coating are applied. But, in writing this…the other variable in the cork, which is mother nature’s molecules…will produce a unique cork every time…I think., i.e., there is built in variablity in the cork bark which didn’t cause problems with parrafin, but might have started problems when coated with the more rigid siicone…or a misture. And, some of those corks are rock hard when you try to squeeze them. Try that with a pre-1900 cork and it’s like night and day.

What a mess…

I think it’s time now that “we” here at WB solve the problem for the industry…since the industry hasn’t been able to solve it in the 20 years since its inception.

This all reminds me of the mayor of Philadelphia when I first moved here…in the mid-70s…and his famous boast: “The streets of Philadelphia are safe; it’s the people who make them unsafe”. Maybe the focus on the streets (the wine) is , as it was for Rizzo, wrong…and it’s really “the people” (the closures and their abilty to keep in the protective SO2) that has taken this quest in the wrong, futile direction. You can’t blame the streets…true…but…focus on who’s on them/in their bottlenecks.

larry schaffer:

Here is a question for those who have been drinking white burgundy’s for a long time.

Did you ever experience a pre mox bottle prior to 1995? We’re all white burgundy’s ageable for decades before then?

There seems to be this inherent expectation that these specific whites will age but very few others will, including most domestic chardonnays. Is this because of history? Experience? I’m just curious as there certainly are plenty of domestic whites that have proven the test of time - try and aged Qupe Marsanne or Roussanne, for instance, or a Mayacamas or Stony Hill or Mt. Eden Chardonnay.

Just an aside . . .

Cheers.

Good evening Larry,

Yes- premox existed before 1995, but in my experience it was either a rare one-off experience or it was heavily attributable to an individual producer. For my part, the only serious premox issues I have had pre-1995 were with Amiot and it affected everything, including a few vintages of Montrachet that should have been in prime condition based on the relative performance of other Montrachets of similarly high caliber.

What is happening now is more widespread, and with the passage of time even more shocking given not only the overall levels of premox, but how it has relatively recently become a major issue at Jadot and Leflaive- two of the best and most expertly managed cellars you will find in all of France- where before it was not the issue it was with other reputable Domaines.

I have read a wide variety of opinions on the matter, and as a layman I have no basis on which to speculate as to root causes, much less solutions.

One thing I will say though- and this is PURELY anecdotal from first hand experience- is that my premox experiences are skewed towards wines that were not verifiably sourced and single-owner cellared from the release date- ie they spent some time in the secondary market.

This is merely a general experience and does not explain the problem. In fact the worst premox situation I have ever seen first hand was an entire 12 bottle case of 2002 Blain-Gagnard Montrachet that completely premoxed within 8 years of release- and that had been sourced by a friend at release and cellared perfectly.

However, I have wondered if perhaps excessive secondary market trading of bottles might possibly exacerbate a problem that already exists. If that were to prove accurate- then I think it would put some part of the blame on cork movement and potential oxygen exposure that is a greater risk when you do not know where a wine has been for X number of years, or for issues in transport etc. In other words, I wonder if excessive exposure from frequent trading or heat exposure (which shows quite differently from premox in and of itself) might trigger something that would otherwise not be triggered or be triggered on a delayed basis if the bottle had been well stored.

I realize how silly this must sound to some of you, but given how long the problem has gone on, the randomness with which it manifests itself and the fact this is happening to some of the most talented winemakers on the planet drives one to take whatever data they have on hand and think about any possibilities that may arise.

It bears remembering as well that pre-mox at current levels has arisen at a time when so many variables changed- a shift in customer demand and changing customer tastes, the emergence of a whole new generation of winemakers as pricing demand created economics that facilitated a lot of vineyards and sharing arrangements changing hands, a worldwide increase in demand on cork as wine overall became more popular, and of course the emergence of a highly liquid secondary market that ensures wider distribution including both the risk of gaps in provenance and the reality of more “eyes on the ball” as it were as the wines age.

John Morris:

That’s an interesting article (through repetitive). It’s interesting that they’ve twice lowered the free sulfur levels at bottling since adopting the Diams.

Fevre has a horrible reputation for premox all the way through the 2009 vintage. We had two this year at the annual dinner (2009 Fevre Clos and Preuses) and Fevre Clos was clearly advanced. According to Steen’s article, Fevre was using 40 ppm (or 40mg/L) as their SO2 level. Steen doesn’t tell us the vintage he’s referring to, but presumably that’s from 2009, the last year the grand cru wines were bottled under conventional corks. But what concerns me is that if Fevre was using 40 ppm of SO2, and they are still having premox in their bottles at 7.5 years in the 2009 vintage – by which time Fevre had supposedly used better corks – it suggests that they had some other significant wine-making problem that was causing premox in their wines.

Assuming that there was no other problem in the winemaking process that was exposing the wines to premature oxidation (such as failure to properly dose the wine with SO2 at the crusher, excessive lees stirring or stirring the lees after the M/L is finished, failure to keep the barrels properly topped up, use of too much new oak (which has a much higher oxygen transmission rate through the staves), injection of dissolved oxygen from modern automated bottling lines/equipment, or exposure to some oxidizer like iron piping somewhere along the line) then maybe the much more uniform and controlled oxygen transmission rate obtainable through the DIAM closure would permit Fevre to safely cut the SO2 back from 40 ppm to 30 ppm. But it seems to me that Fevre is eliminating their safety margin and there is certainly ample reason to think, based on the 2009 vintage as well as all of the vintages before it, that Fevre had a much bigger winemaking problem rather than just problems with their corks. I can tell you that the 2015s from Fevre certainly did not inspire my two colleagues from the annual premox dinners that tasted them both in London in January and March here in LA.

Assuming that there was no other problem in the winemaking process that was exposing the wines to premature oxidation (such as failure to properly dose the wine with SO2 at the crusher, excessive lees stirring or stirring the lees after the M/L is finished, failure to keep the barrels properly topped up, use of too much new oak (which has a much higher oxygen transmission rate through the staves), injection of dissolved oxygen from modern automated bottling lines/equipment, or exposure to some oxidizer like iron piping somewhere along the line) then maybe the much more uniform and controlled oxygen transmission rate obtainable through the DIAM closure would permit Fevre to safely cut the SO2 back from 40 ppm to 30 ppm. But it seems to me that Fevre is eliminating their safety margin and there is certainly ample reason to think, based on the 2009 vintage as well as all of the vintages before it, that Fevre had a much bigger winemaking problem rather than just problems with their corks. I can tell you that the 2015s from Fevre certainly did not inspire my two colleagues from the annual premox dinners that tasted them both in London in January and March here in LA.

I stopped buying Fevre for this very reason.
I agree that there must be issues other than closures.

posts.name: Don Cornwell
posts.cooked:
Mel Knox:

Don,
The oxygen transmitted to wine from barrels comes from within the staves,
Not through the staves.

I don t think this amount is statistically significant.

Mel:

Oxygen does in fact permeate through the staves. See http://pubs.acs.org/doi/abs/10.1021/jf505360r and Ratiometric Oxygen Imaging to Predict Oxygen Diffusivity in Oak Wood During Red Wine Barrel Aging | Food and Bioprocess Technology andhttp://www.infowine.com/en/video/oak_barrel_and_oxygen_comparisons_facts_and_hypotheses_sc_13124.htm and the degree of toast etc also has an effect on permeability. Somewhere I have a copy of a study done a few years ago that showed that new barrels are much more oxygen permeable than older ones, which presumably absorb some of the wine they are holding over time.

One of the points raised in Steen’s article in passing is the so-called “Roulot method.” (I’m not quite sure why Jean-Marc is given credit for it, because I think that Raveneau was the first to use it, but I’ll leave that aside.) The method is simple. The wine is only allowed to remain in barrel for 11 or 12 months and spends its remaining time before bottling in tank, or in the case of Domaine Leflaive, at least before 2002, in small steel vessels that were like double-sized barrels. Many of the producers who use this method are among the group with the lowest incidence of premox since the year 2000, including Raveneau, Roulot, Jean-Marc Pillot, Jacques Carillon, and at least formerly, Domaine Leflaive. (I suspect, but have no confirmation, that eliminating this was one of the changes made by Eric Remy at Leflaive). Those first four names alone should should get people’s attention. It certainly strongly suggests that limiting the amount of aging in barrel to 11 or 12 months helps to reduce the oxidation potential.

William:

My impression about Leflaive’s SO2 usage is the same as yours. One of the things that really impressed me when I looked at Leflaive’s wine-making process was that Pierre Morey monitored the SO2 levels throughout the wine-making process and made small additions of SO2 as needed throughout the process. That was a very unusual practice in burgundy at that time, where most wines saw no SO2 additions between the crush pad and bottling and almost no one else monitored SO2 levels on an ongoing basis. On the other hand, California winemakers would all tell you that what Leflaive did is pretty basic wine making. (That’s just part of the difference in what winemakers are taught in US versus Burgundy and we put a much greater emphasis on scientific measurement and having at least a basic understanding the underlying chemistry here. But since the Burgundians historically had a natural advantage in terms of generally higher total acidity levels and lower ph, they also, without knowing it, had naturally higher levels of molecular free SO2, which is ph-coupled and rises as ph falls. But global warming is changing that. The Burgundians, who saw average sugar levels rise and average total acidity levels fall over the last 15 years (according to the BIVB records), were frequently facing more California-like fruit and I don’t think they ever realized that they now had wines that really needed ongoing SO2 monitoring and adjustments if necessary. But I digress…)

I’m not sure what happened with Leflaive’s 2002s. It was the first vintage in Morey’s tenure where there was any level of premox. It was maybe 1 out of every 12 to 15 bottles, and it didn’t start showing up until the wines were about nine years old, but it definitely happened.

Then I think Leflaive got scared by what happened to them in the 2004 vintage, which experienced the most severe oidium problems for white burgundy in the modern era. Everyone dusted their vines and fruit with elemental sulfur every two weeks for several months. No one seems to have realized, until after the wines were well into the fermentation process, that a lot of sulfur was trapped within the bunches. The result was that everyone made wines reductively in 2004. For Leflaive, who apparently didn’t adjust their usual techniques, they ended up with super-reductive wines in 2004. A few of the Laflaive cuvees, particularly 2004 Clavoillon, ended up being permanently reductive and in some instances exhibiting mercaptans – in a nutshell, the wine was completely undrinkable. I also opened a couple of Batards that exhibited mercaptans as well. The 2004 Leflaive grand crus always struck me as very hard and somewhat harsh wines and there was no escaping the fact that they were quite reductive. I still have couple of bottles of the Chevalier left just for monitoring purposes.

I surmise that the 2004 vintage, along with Anne-Claude’s growing fixation on method bio-dynamie, caused Leflaive to lower their use of SO2 and, as you say, become less concerned about monitoring of the wines, topping up the casks, and adjusting SO2 throughout the wine-making process. The most recent figures I’ve seen on Leflaive’s free SO2 targets for bottling – at 25 to 28 ppm – are among the lowest in burgundy today. Obviously, something has substantially changed over time because Leflaive went from being one of the five best producers from a premox perspective from the 1995 to 2001 vintages to one of the ten worst producers from a premox perspective from the 2006 to 2009 vintages.

Don:

That analysis sounds very convincing to me.

The Leflaive wines present a real conundrum to review, since they taste impeccably classic from tank (living up, I would say, to their reputation) but come with such a high probability of crashing-and-burning in the cellar. The raw materials are great but how does one assess a compromised product?

To pursue your digression on the subject of pH, the paradox seems to me to be that high acid vintages (e.g. 1996, 2008) don’t seem to be appreciably less susceptible to premox than lower-acid vintages (e.g. 2006, 2005). I have sometimes wondered whether malolactic stability is one further piece of the premox puzzle: in vintages with high levels of malic acid and sluggish malolactic fermentations, did wines go to bottle when they were still a little unstable—even if, analytically, all the malic acid was gone? If they did then malolactic bacterial activity would presumably burn through the wines’ SO2 pretty rapidly, leaving you, effectively, with vin nature. Alternatively, the techniques necessary to force through malolactic fermentation in time for bottling when wines are high in malic acid and low in pH, e.g. warming cellars, stirring, may also be culprits.

Again, since most roads lead to oxidation, 2005s and 2008s may be premoxed for different reasons.

posts.name: Don Cornwell
posts.cooked:
William Kelley:

To pursue your digression on the subject of pH, the paradox seems to me to be that high acid vintages (e.g. 1996, 2008) don’t seem to be appreciably less susceptible to premox than lower-acid vintages (e.g. 2006, 2005). I have sometimes wondered whether malolactic stability is one further piece of the premox puzzle: in vintages with high levels of malic acid and sluggish malolactic fermentations, did wines go to bottle when they were still a little unstable—even if, analytically, all the malic acid was gone? If they did then malolactic bacterial activity would presumably burn through the wines’ SO2 pretty rapidly, leaving you, effectively, with vin nature. Alternatively, the techniques necessary to force through malolactic fermentation in time for bottling when wines are high in malic acid and low in pH, e.g. warming cellars, stirring, may also be culprits.

Again, since most roads lead to oxidation, 2005s and 2008s may be premoxed for different reasons.

I think it is difficult to compare 1996 with the more recent vintages because a lot of things were done differently in 1996 versus now. For example, in 1996 some producers admitted that they cut their SO2 usage even more than they had for 1995 because they thought the wines would taste too severe on release due to the very high acidity, and producers broke all sorts of records with respect to the amount of open top lees stirring that they did trying to “fatten up” the wines. Based on what’s been said by all of the producers, everyone seems to have learned from those particular mistakes.

However, I agree that absent other explanatory factors, one would notionally expect that the higher acid vintages should perform better on average from a premox perspective than lower acid vintages and yes, you’re right that the outcome data from my annual dinners doesn’t really match this rule of thumb. 2005, which had the lowest overall average total acidity at harvest in the last 30+ years (about 4.5 g/L), did have the highest premox incidence we’ve experienced to date (31%). So the 2005 vintage seems consistent with the expected outcome from the rule of thumb. But 2009, which had total acidity on average of 5.4 g/L, and very comparable high levels of sugar (203 g/L for 2009 vs 205 g/L for 2005) was one of the the lowest premox incidence vintages at just under 13%. 2008 was one of the higher acid vintages (with 7.4 g/L average TA while and sugar on average of 185 g/L), but that vintage had a 17% premox incidence – higher than in 2007 or 2009.

As you say, other factors may be in play in the outlier vintages. For example, there were problems with surmaturite in 2008 and rot in a few cases (grey rot is known to cause premox and is the suspected culprit along with lower SO2 levels on the 1995s.) With respect to 2009, it is quite possible, and perhaps probable, that the lower premox percentage is partially due to the fact that I made a concerted effort to include all of the 2009s bottled with DIAM that I could get my hands on. We had a total of nine bottles (of 62 total) that were closed with DIAM. None of the DIAM bottles were advanced or oxidized. If I excluded those nine bottles from the calculations, the premox percentage of the non-DIAM bottles increases from 13% to 15% – but’s still below the 17% figure we had for the higher acid 2008 vintage.

So, yes, there are obviously some other pathways to premox (or avoiding premox) here and higher acidity alone doesn’t necessarily mean there will be less premox in that vintage.

My chemistry knowledge currently isn’t deep enough to understand what happens with SO2 in the presence of malic acidity vs. lactic acidity. However, one of the ironies here is that with Jadot, which historically blocked part of the M/L, I experienced no premox on any of the bottles I opened from the 1995, 1996 and 1999 vintages until the wines were 12 years old. (Then in 2000 the tidal wave of premoxed bottles from Jadot began.) Steve Tanzer wrote early on about your instablity point. Steve felt that some of the producers, particularly in Chassagne, who typically bottled most of their wines 11 months after the vintage (in order to clear space in the winery for the next vintage), were forced to bottle the wines in some vintages when the M/L was maybe not 100% complete or where the wines had only completed the M/L one to two months before and he thought that they just weren’t stable enough from an SO2 and acidity perspective because they were forced to rush the bottling. Steve felt that this contributed to premox and that if these producers were able to extend the elevage and keep the wines in barrel longer they would be much more stable and better able to withstand premox. Niellon was his prime example. In Niellon’s case, he moved into his new expanded size facility before the 2009 vintage and he extended the elevage to 18 months. So, sure enough, the 2009 Batard from Niellon at our dinner in March was one of the clearly advanced wines of the vintage. We didn’t get to taste the 2009 Chevalier due to a mixup, but I have a bottle in my cellar that I’m waiting to try.

Oxidation is not a simple subject, that’s for sure. The more I learn, the more anomalies I find in the overall data and I realize that I still have a lot more to learn.

posts.name: Alan Rath
posts.cooked:
Don Cornwell:

My chemistry knowledge currently isn’t deep enough to understand what happens with SO2 in the presence of malic acidity vs. lactic acidity. However, one of the ironies here is that with Jadot, which historically blocked part of the M/L, I experienced no premox on any of the bottles I opened from the 1995, 1996 and 1999 vintages until the wines were 12 years old.

Don, the very simple description is that malic acid has a pKa of 3.4, and lactic 3.86. This means that malic is a “stronger” acid than lactic, i.e., for the same concentration, there will be more free H+ ions from malic than from lactic. And that pushes the balance of SO2 <> HSO3- toward more SO2, which supposedly should help protect the wine better. Beyond that, I can’t help interpret the relationship between malolactic percentage and likelihood of premox.

posts.name: larry schaffer
posts.cooked:
Alan Rath:
Don Cornwell:

My chemistry knowledge currently isn’t deep enough to understand what happens with SO2 in the presence of malic acidity vs. lactic acidity. However, one of the ironies here is that with Jadot, which historically blocked part of the M/L, I experienced no premox on any of the bottles I opened from the 1995, 1996 and 1999 vintages until the wines were 12 years old.

Don, the very simple description is that malic acid has a pKa of 3.4, and lactic 3.86. This means that malic is a “stronger” acid than lactic, i.e., for the same concentration, there will be more free H+ ions from malic than from lactic. And that pushes the balance of SO2 <> HSO3- toward more SO2, which supposedly should help protect the wine better. Beyond that, I can’t help interpret the relationship between malolactic percentage and likelihood of premox.

A couple of other points here:

  1. When a winery ‘partially blocks’ ML, one has to ask how. Are they using lysozyme to kill the possibility of the process taking part, or simply hitting the wine with a larger dose of SO2, which is the more common and less invasive way of doing so?

  2. If ML is blocked, is this winery filtering their wines prior to bottling? I have no idea how much filtration takes place with these wines but if ML is blocked, you’d be worried about the possibility of secondary fermentation if the wine is not filtered.

  3. When ML is blocked, as Alan points out, the remaining wine does have a lower pH than if the wine had gone through ML. This ‘shift’ is variable depending upon the level of ML in the wine to start with, the variety, etc.

And if a wine has a lower pH, you would theoretically need less SO2 in order to maintain an ‘anti-oxidative’ environment compared to a wine with a higher pH.

Cheers

Stuart BeauneHead Niemtzow:

I see we’re totally focused on the wines/winemaking again here.

To flip the analyis a little:

If it’s the winemaking…too much malo/too little, etc. how do you explain perfectly great bottles of Niellon which I had last week from the 1999 vintage…after a couple of oxidized bottles in the last few years, ie, with little hope…and pleasant surprise.

Whatever percentage Don and his dinner extravaganzas are finding…of oxidized…why focus only on them…instead of mainly on the good bottles? At this point good bottles might be more challenging to explain with all the experimenting.

Focusing on the good bottles seems like it might be more fruitful at this point…and explaining them. And, there are some bottles that have aged just as well as WB from the '70s and '80s…We’re treating them as the freaks, but maybe they’re not…and the wine is fine.

Maybe, just maybe all the problems are from what it done after the wine leaves the bottling machine…whatever the problems are.

Let’s say a wine from Neillon needs 99 units of oxygen exchanged (i’m making up a number and terms) to oxidize. Each cork has a different oxygen exchange rate, so some would reach that number faster and some would reach it slower. But that 99 units is because of the winemaking.

and if one cork would let through 50 units and another 150 units, then the failure of one bottle is a combination of cork and winemaking.

how 'bout, as Sauzet/Boudot experienced…all of the SO2 was gone from the affected bottles and all of the SO2 was still in the unaffected bottles. Is that still the winemaking? It seems that the winemaking is plenty good enough…with the intended protection still in the bottle, rather than gone 3 weeks after bottling. I guess you couild argue that the SO2 treatment is part of the winemaking, but…a part certainly done right.

Like I said… after all this inconclusive b.s. , after 20 years, I’m moving onto focusing on the good bottles, not the bad ones. They are aberrations for some reason.And, simply put, if a wine is good in many instances, how can the winemaking be the problem…or even part of it.

And., I’m now focused on the transfer of SO2 OUT, not the ingestion of oxygen. Oxygen is present when the wine is bottled and controlled oxidation is the traditional way wines age.

posts.name: Robert Grenley
posts.cooked:
Don Cornwell:
William Kelley:
Don Cornwell:

Leflaive still put the wines into steel after 10-12 months, incidentally: I tasted the '15s from tank in October. My intuition (I speculate) is that the problems there stem from low sulfur and perhaps a laissez faire attitude to topping, inspired by some of the more extreme aspects of the bio-dynamic philosophy. There are, after all, several different routes to prematurely oxidized wines.

William:

My impression about Leflaive’s SO2 usage is the same as yours. One of the things that really impressed me when I looked at Leflaive’s wine-making process was that Pierre Morey monitored the SO2 levels throughout the wine-making process and made small additions of SO2 as needed throughout the process. That was a very unusual practice in burgundy at that time, where most wines saw no SO2 additions between the crush pad and bottling and almost no one else monitored SO2 levels on an ongoing basis. On the other hand, California winemakers would all tell you that what Leflaive did is pretty basic wine making. (That’s just part of the difference in what winemakers are taught in US versus Burgundy and we put a much greater emphasis on scientific measurement and having at least a basic understanding the underlying chemistry here. But since the Burgundians historically had a natural advantage in terms of generally higher total acidity levels and lower ph, they also, without knowing it, had naturally higher levels of molecular free SO2, which is ph-coupled and rises as ph falls. But global warming is changing that. The Burgundians, who saw average sugar levels rise and average total acidity levels fall over the last 15 years (according to the BIVB records), were frequently facing more California-like fruit and I don’t think they ever realized that they now had wines that really needed ongoing SO2 monitoring and adjustments if necessary. But I digress…)

I’m not sure what happened with Leflaive’s 2002s. It was the first vintage in Morey’s tenure where there was any level of premox. It was maybe 1 out of every 12 to 15 bottles, and it didn’t start showing up until the wines were about nine years old, but it definitely happened.

Then I think Leflaive got scared by what happened to them in the 2004 vintage, which experienced the most severe oidium problems for white burgundy in the modern era. Everyone dusted their vines and fruit with elemental sulfur every two weeks for several months. No one seems to have realized, until after the wines were well into the fermentation process, that a lot of sulfur was trapped within the bunches. The result was that everyone made wines reductively in 2004. For Leflaive, who apparently didn’t adjust their usual techniques, they ended up with super-reductive wines in 2004. A few of the Laflaive cuvees, particularly 2004 Clavoillon, ended up being permanently reductive and in some instances exhibiting mercaptans – in a nutshell, the wine was completely undrinkable. I also opened a couple of Batards that exhibited mercaptans as well. The 2004 Leflaive grand crus always struck me as very hard and somewhat harsh wines and there was no escaping the fact that they were quite reductive. I still have couple of bottles of the Chevalier left just for monitoring purposes.

I surmise that the 2004 vintage, along with Anne-Claude’s growing fixation on method bio-dynamie, caused Leflaive to lower their use of SO2 and, as you say, become less concerned about monitoring of the wines, topping up the casks, and adjusting SO2 throughout the wine-making process. The most recent figures I’ve seen on Leflaive’s free SO2 targets for bottling – at 25 to 28 ppm – are among the lowest in burgundy today. Obviously, something has substantially changed over time because Leflaive went from being one of the five best producers from a premox perspective from the 1995 to 2001 vintages to one of the ten worst producers from a premox perspective from the 2006 to 2009 vintages.

Excellent information. Finally explains why my first premoxed Leflaives were 2002’s, why my 2004’s were reduced and the Clavoillons were undrinkable, why the vintages since have been “different” upon release, and why Leflaive has gone from my “go to” producer to my "run away from"producer.

posts.name: John Morris
posts.cooked:
Mel - As far as I can make out, Stuart seems to be contending that if there’s bottle variation “the cause” of premox must be the cork, whereas most people here seem to think it’s likely that (a) changes in winemaking made the wines more vulnerable to oxidation and (b) inherent variations in the permeability of corks, or some change in the corks, results in the randomness.

I seems likely me that both are necessary conditions for premox but neither alone is a sufficient condition. Saying one or the other is “the cause” just confuses matters.
